steps.title: Find the structural relationship being tested
steps.content:
The main clause states “it was not universally embraced,” but what follows immediately contradicts or clarifies what not universal means.

The “whereas” clause shows the divided response: some people “flocked to balloon launches and purchased balloon-themed items,” while others “dismissed the hot-air balloon as an impractical and dangerous extravagance.” The contrast between embrace and dismissal is the key signal that proves the embrace was not universal.

steps.title: Choose punctuation that marks explanation
steps.content:
A colon introduces an explanation or elaboration of what comes before it.

The colon between “embraced” and “whereas many flocked…, others dismissed…” signals that the contrast statement that follows explains what “not universally embraced” actually means—some people did embrace it, but others rejected it, which proves the embrace was divided, not universal.

A comma (C) would merely separate clauses; no punctuation (B) creates awkward flow; and “and” (D) does not pair grammatically with “whereas.” The colon explicitly marks that what follows elaborates the preceding claim.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
